topic: Vector Database Comparison 2026: Pinecone vs Weaviate vs Chroma vs pgvector

competitors.title: Pinecone vs Weaviate vs Chroma vs pgvector (2026) - stackviv.ai
key_points: Pinecone wins for production RAG systems with zero operational headaches and consistent sub-50ms latency at scale
Weaviate is the hybrid search champion, combining vector similarity with keyword matching and knowledge graph features
Chroma works best for prototyping and smaller projects under 1 million vectors, especially if you're already in Python
pgvector makes sense when you already run PostgreSQL and want to keep vectors alongside your relational data
Pinecone's p99 latency stays around 47ms at 1 billion 768-dimension vectors
Serverless pricing: $0.33 per GB storage, $8.25 per million read units, $2 per million write units
Pinecone Starter plan free with 2GB storage; Standard plans start at $50/month minimum; Enterprise plans require $500/month minimum
Weaviate Cloud starts at $25/month after 14-day trial with latencies around 123ms at billion-vector scale
Chroma completed Rust-core rewrite in 2025 delivering 4x faster writes and queries
pgvector with pgvectorscale achieves 471 QPS at 99% recall on 50 million vectors

competitors.title: Best Vector Databases in 2026: Complete Comparison Guide – Encore
key_points: pgvector: PostgreSQL extension, best for teams already running Postgres, works with workloads under 5 million vectors with 5-50ms latency
Pinecone: Managed SaaS, zero-ops serverless, scales to billions of vectors
Qdrant: Dedicated vector DB written in Rust, open-source, scales to hundreds of millions of vectors with payload filtering
Weaviate: Dedicated vector DB with built-in vectorization modules, scales to hundreds of millions of vectors
Milvus: Dedicated vector DB for enterprise scale with GPU acceleration, scales to billions of vectors
Chroma: Embedded/client-server, best for prototyping and local development, scales to hundreds of thousands of vectors
LanceDB: Embedded with zero-copy columnar storage, scales to millions of vectors
Decision matrix: Use pgvector if already on Postgres with <5M vectors; Pinecone for zero ops at any scale; Qdrant for open-source self-hosted; Weaviate for built-in vectorization and hybrid search

competitors.title: Vector Databases Compared 2026: Pinecone vs Qdrant vs Weaviate vs Chroma vs pgvector
key_points: Pinecone: Managed cloud, production-ready, serverless pricing consumption-based (storage per GB, reads/writes per million operations)
Qdrant: High-performance Rust-based, open-source, free tier at 1GB storage, paid plans from $25/month
Weaviate: GraphQL API, multi-modal, hybrid search, managed hosting starting around $25/month
Chroma: Developer-first, local/embedded, easiest for prototyping, less suitable for production scale
pgvector: Postgres extension, simplest if already using PostgreSQL, competitive for moderate datasets (<10M vectors)
Embedding 1M documents with OpenAI's text-embedding-3-small costs $10-20, storage about 6GB raw data (15-30GB with indexes)
Pinecone serverless storage for 1M vector index: $1-5/month
Self-hosted Qdrant on 16GB RAM VM: $50-100/month infrastructure costs
Head-to-head comparison table: Pinecone (managed only), Qdrant (self-host or cloud), Weaviate (self-host or cloud), Chroma (embedded or cloud), pgvector (self-host/managed Postgres)

angles: Performance vs. convenience trade-off: Pinecone offers managed ease while pgvector provides PostgreSQL integration
Open-source vs. managed solutions: Qdrant and Weaviate offer open-source flexibility vs Pinecone's managed service
Hybrid search capabilities: Weaviate leads with built-in hybrid vector+keyword search
Integration with existing infrastructure: pgvector leverages existing PostgreSQL investments
Cost considerations at scale: Serverless pricing models vs self-hosted infrastructure costs
Future trends: Multi-modal search, longer context windows, and AI agent integration
